title: Slow-cooker chicken cacciatore
subtitle: Chicken thighs with tomatoes, olives, and oregano
excerpt: Chicken thighs braised with tinned tomatoes, black olives, red wine, and oregano for six hours until the sauce is thick and intensely flavoured. A straightforward Italian braise that requires ten minutes of prep and no further attention. Serve with pasta or crusty bread.
sourceNotes: Cacciatore means 'hunter's style' in Italian — a method of braising meat with tomatoes, olives, and herbs that is common across central Italy. The slow cooker version became popular in British home cooking because it requires minimal skill and produces a deeply flavoured sauce. Bone-in chicken thighs are traditional and produce the best result; boneless thighs can be substituted but the sauce will be less rich.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes well for up to 3 months.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Better the next day. The sauce thickens and deepens as it rests.

content.text: Use bone-in, skin-on chicken thighs if possible. The bone adds body to the sauce over the long cook and the skin, even though it will not crisp in the slow cooker, renders fat into the sauce. Brown the thighs skin-side down before they go in for the best result.

Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} in a wide pan over high heat. Season the {{chicken-thigh}} with {{sea-salt-fine}} and {{black-pepper}}. Brown skin-side down for 4 minutes until golden. Transfer to the slow cooker. Add the {{onion}} to the pan and fry for 2 minutes. Add the {{garlic}} and {{tomato-puree}} and cook for 1 minute. Pour in the {{red-wine}} and let it bubble briefly. Pour over the chicken. Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}}, {{olives-black}}, {{oregano-dried}}, and {{bay-leaves}}. Cook on low for 6 to 8 hours, until the chicken is tender and the sauce is thick. Remove the {{bay-leaves}} before serving.

items.cause: Red wine not allowed to bubble off before going in
items.fix: Always let the wine bubble for at least 1 minute in the pan before adding it to the slow cooker. This cooks off the sharpest alcohol

items.cause: Olives in brine rather than in oil
items.fix: Rinse brine-packed olives in cold water before adding. Oil-packed olives can go in as they are

items.cause: Too much liquid added, or lid not sealing
items.fix: Remove the lid for the last 30 minutes on high to reduce. The sauce should be thick enough to coat a spoon

content.text: Chicken cacciatore is a weeknight Italian braise that suits the slow cooker completely. The olives and tomatoes produce enough liquid to braise the chicken without adding stock. Serve with pasta or polenta, and spoon the sauce generously. A green salad alongside cuts through the richness.
